title: What Are We Building?
intro: Before coding anything, let's make sure you understand what a task manager actually needs. Think of an MVP like a taco stand before a full restaurant — you don't need valet parking on day one, just great tacos. Swipe right for features that belong in our MVP, left for ones that don't.

teach.title: Welcome to Your First Build
teach.body: Over the next 9 chapters, you'll build a real task manager app from scratch. Not a tutorial where you copy-paste code — you'll make decisions, write prompts, review code, and ship something real.

teach.title: MVP = Skateboard, Not a Wheel
teach.body: An MVP (Minimum Viable Product) isn't half a car — it's a skateboard. It's the smallest thing that actually works and solves a problem. Our task manager MVP needs to let users create, complete, and organize tasks. That's it. No AI summaries, no team collaboration, no Gantt charts. Those come later.

teach.title: The Taco Stand Before the Restaurant
teach.body: Think of building an MVP like opening a taco stand before a full restaurant. You don't need a 50-page menu, a sommelier, and valet parking on day one. You need great tacos, a way to take orders, and happy customers. Once people are lining up for your tacos, THEN you expand. Our task manager is that taco stand — simple, focused, and ready to serve.

Every successful SaaS product started as a simple MVP. The task manager you're about to build is the kind of app people pay $5-10/month for — and you're learning to build it with AI in a fraction of the time.
A task manager MVP needs: create tasks, mark them complete, organize by priority or date. Everything else is a 'nice to have' that can wait.

cards.content: Users can create a task with a title and optional description
cards.explanation: This is the core feature. Without creating tasks, there's no task manager.

cards.content: Users can mark a task as done with a checkbox
cards.explanation: Completing tasks is the whole point. This is essential for an MVP.

cards.content: AI generates a daily summary of your productivity
cards.explanation: Cool feature, but way too complex for an MVP. This requires an AI integration, usage tracking, and more. Save it for v2.

cards.content: Users can delete tasks they no longer need
cards.explanation: Basic CRUD. If users can create tasks, they need to be able to remove them.

cards.content: Share tasks with team members and assign them to people
cards.explanation: Collaboration is a huge feature that requires user management, permissions, notifications, and more. Not MVP material.

cards.content: Set a due date on tasks
cards.explanation: Simple and useful. Due dates help users prioritize and are easy to implement with a date picker.

cards.content: Visualize tasks on a Gantt chart timeline
cards.explanation: Gantt charts are complex UI components. Way beyond MVP scope. A simple list sorted by date works fine.
